theme: The Floor Is Alive — Make Every Surface React, Every Click Speak, Every Second Count

ideas.title: Worker Click Quips — Characters Who Talk Back
ideas.description: Clicking an active worker on the office floor currently jumps straight to the Dashboard tab. That's functional but cold. Instead, fire a 2-second speech bubble from that worker's avatar FIRST — a status-specific one-liner — then navigate. Each worker gets their own voice: Maya clicking at 'working' → 'Hot take incoming. Reviewing in 5.' Carlos at 'error' → 'The logs lie. I'm looking into it.' Dave at 'idle' → 'Pipeline's building. Touch grass window is open.' Sofia at 'working' → 'Test coverage? We don't talk about test coverage.' Implementation: in Character.jsx, intercept the onClick prop, fire a local `clickQuote` state that shows the bubble for 1800ms (using setTimeout), then call the original onWorkerClick. Define a `WORKER_QUIPS` const of `{ workerId: { statusKey: string[] } }` — 2-3 lines each. WHY: The office floor currently rewards clicking with a tab switch. That's a navigation event, not a character moment. A single line of dialogue transforms a UI interaction into a personality encounter. It makes each worker feel like a real person, not a status tile.

ideas.title: Vibe-Reactive Floor Grid Glow — The Room Breathes the Vibe
ideas.description: The office floor grid lines are always the same dim dark color regardless of whether the team is crushing it or melting down. Add `data-vibe={vibe}` to the `.office-floor` element and drive a `--grid-line-color` CSS variable per vibe state: 'crushing' → subtle violet/purple glow lines that pulse slowly (0.6 opacity, box-shadow on the floor container), 'on-fire' → faint red-orange grid with a slight warm glow, 'in-flow' → cool teal, 'slow-day' → nearly invisible grey (barely there), 'after-hours' → moonlight blue with reduced opacity. The grid itself is already rendered via `background-image` in Office.css — just override the color variable. Implementation: one attribute added in Office.jsx (`data-vibe={vibe}` on `.office-floor`), five CSS overrides using `[data-vibe='crushing'] .office-floor { --grid-color: rgba(139,92,246,0.18); }`. WHY: The vibe pill and Bloberto's face both react to team state, but the floor — which takes up 70% of the screen — stays static. When the team is crushing it, the ROOM itself should glow. Right now the most reactive element is a pill in the header. Flip that.

ideas.title: New Hire Walks In Through the Entrance — Arrivals as Events
ideas.description: The entrance door already exists at the bottom of the office floor (`.office-door`, rendered in Office.jsx). When the polling diff detects a first-time worker (`firstTime` array in App.jsx, already computed), that worker should appear at the entrance position — not their desk — and animate to their desk over 1.5 seconds. Implementation: in Office.jsx, accept a `newArrivals` prop (Set of worker IDs that just appeared for the first time). When rendering a Character whose ID is in `newArrivals`, start their CSS `left`/`top` at the entrance coordinates (~50%, 88%) with a 1.5s CSS transition that moves them to their desk position. Use a `useEffect` that removes the ID from the local entrance-animation set after the transition completes. WHY: Right now the activity log says '🟢 Maya joined' but the office floor just... suddenly has Maya at her desk. The door already exists. The hire event is already tracked. The transition cost is one CSS animation. Watching someone walk in through the front door — even at 40px scale — is a completely different emotional experience than a grid tile appearing.

ideas.title: Vibe-Reactive 'Boss' Honorific — The Title Earns Its Keep
ideas.description: The header greeting reads '{time greeting}, boss' on every single page load forever. The word 'boss' is hardcoded as a `<span>`. Make it vibe-reactive: 'crushing' → 'LEGEND 🔥', 'on-fire' → 'captain 🚨', 'in-flow' → 'boss', 'slow-day' → 'chief... you still there? 😐', 'after-hours' → 'night owl 🌙'. Implementation: replace the hardcoded `boss` string with a lookup: `const honorific = { crushing: 'LEGEND 🔥', 'on-fire': 'captain 🚨', 'in-flow': 'boss', 'slow-day': 'chief... you still there? 😐', 'after-hours': 'night owl 🌙' }[teamVibe.key] ?? 'boss'` in App.jsx. The `<span>` wrapper already exists for styling. WHY: The greeting is the FIRST thing you read on every visit. Right now it's a timestamp with a generic honorific. During a 'crushing' sprint, being greeted as 'LEGEND' hits different from 'boss'. It costs 5 lines of code and returns a persistent moment of delight on every page load. It's the cheapest personalization this app can do.

ideas.title: Bloberto's Live Clock + Sync Countdown on Manager Desk
ideas.description:
Bloberto's manager desk has a monitor element (`mgr-desk__monitor`) and a vibe nameplate, but shows no live information. Add a small HH:MM clock inside the manager desk area, updated every minute via a `useEffect` interval in Office.jsx. Directly beneath or beside it, show the next-sync countdown: '⟳ 28s' (passed as a `nextSyncIn` prop from App.jsx, derived from `POLL_INTERVAL_ACTIVE - timeSinceLastSync`). Style as a monospace LCD-aesthetic micro-label inside the desk box. Implementation: `
{currentTime}
` and `
⟳ {nextSyncIn}s
` within the manager desk JSX. WHY: The manager desk is the spatial command center of the office but currently shows only static content. A ticking clock and a countdown turn it into a live readout — you feel the rhythm of polling cycles. It also gives impatient users a reason not to hammer the manual sync button: they can SEE the refresh is coming in 12 seconds.

ideas.title: Session Duration Self-Roast in the Footer
ideas.description: The footer says 'v1.0.0-chaos'. Add a live 'Session: 42m' counter right next to it — captured from a `mountTimeRef = useRef(Date.now())` in App.jsx, updated by the existing `useTimeTick` hook every 60 seconds. The commentary escalates with time: 0–29m → plain 'Session: Nm'; 30–59m → 'Session: Nm 🫠'; 60–119m → 'Session: 1h Nm — maybe get some water.'; 120m+ → 'Session: Nh Nm — Bloberto is worried about you.'. Implementation: a `formatSessionTime(ms)` utility function, rendered in the footer `<span>`. WHY: This is pure Bloberto humor. The app is already built on mild existential dread — the footer tagline changes with vibe, the version is 'v1.0.0-chaos'. A session timer that starts roasting you after an hour is perfectly on brand. It's also a subtle accessibility nudge to take breaks. Zero infrastructure cost, infinite personality dividend.

ideas.title: The Entrance Door Has Opinions — Arrival and Departure Reactions
ideas.description: The entrance door (`office-door` in Office.jsx, already rendered at the bottom of the floor) is currently pure scenery. Make it a reactive prop: (a) When a hire/arrival event fires, the door briefly glows green and shows a '👋 Welcome!' tooltip for 2 seconds — achieved by passing a `latestHireTimestamp` prop and triggering a CSS class `office-door--arrival` with a `@keyframes` green pulse; (b) when a worker disappears (done/departed, the `disappeared` array in App.jsx), the door pulses orange ('👋 See ya!') for 2 seconds; (c) during 'after-hours' vibe with 0 active workers, add a small '🔒 CLOSED' badge below the door label. Implementation: pass `doorEvent: { type: 'arrive'|'depart'|null, ts: number }` as a prop to Office.jsx, reset to null after 2000ms. The door component applies CSS classes based on this prop. WHY: Every office has a front door that witnesses comings and goings. Right now ours is furniture. Making the entrance door react to arrivals and departures creates a powerful spatial metaphor that no data panel or activity log can replicate — you literally see people walking in and out of the building. It makes the floor map feel like a place, not a diagram.
